elements.sent-2411-001: Many concurrent algorithms require processes to perform fetch-and-add operations on a single memory location, which can be a hot spot of contention.
elements.sent-2411-002: We present a novel algorithm called {\em Aggregating Funnels that reduces this contention by spreading the fetch-and-add operations across multiple memory locations.}
elements.sent-2411-003: It aggregates fetch-and-add operations into batches so that the batch can be performed by a single hardware fetch-and-add instruction on one location and all operations in the batch can efficiently compute their results by performing a fetch-and-add instruction on a different location.
elements.sent-2411-004: We show experimentally that this approach achieves higher throughput than previous combining techniques, such as Combining Funnels, and is substantially more scalable than applying hardware fetch-and-add instructions on a single memory location.
elements.sent-2411-005: We show that replacing the fetch-and-add instructions in the fastest state-of-the-art concurrent queue by our Aggregating Funnels eliminates a bottleneck and greatly improves the queue's overall throughput.
elements.sent-2411-006: Many concurrent algorithms use fetch-and-add to coordinate the actions of multiple processes.
elements.sent-2411-007: A fetch-and-add on a memory location $X$ {\em atomically adds a given value to $X$ and returns the value that was stored in $X$ before the addition.}
elements.sent-2411-008: Introduced by Gottlieb and Kruskal~\cite{GK81 , fetch-and-add is widely available as a hardware {\em primitive \cite{X86 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Applications often have hot spots of contention where many processes perform concurrent fetch-and-add primitives on the same location, which degrades performance.
elements.sent-2411-010: To mitigate this problem, we introduce Aggregating Funnels, which provide a software implementation of fetch-and-add that is much more scalable than the hardware primitive, and more efficient than state-of-the-art software implementations.
elements.sent-2411-011: Scalable and efficient software replacements for hardware \FAA\ are crucial for the performance of many concurrent algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-012: Applications of \FAA\ include allocating memory addresses for objects of varying size from a block of memory \cite{Sto82,EO88,Wil88 , solving the readers-writers problem \cite{GLR83 , and wait-free universal constructions~\cite{FK14 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: These primitives themselves have a plethora of applications.
elements.sent-2411-014: For example, \op{Fetch\&Inc is used in assigning distinct identifiers to processes \cite{MA95 , reference counting for garbage collection in concurrent systems \cite{Val95,MS95,Sun05 , assigning distinct tickets in a ticket lock \cite{FLBB79,RK79,MS91 , assigning distinct timestamps to operations \cite{BJW23 , and implementing simple barriers~\cite[Chapter 18.2--18.3]{HSLS21 ), array-based queue locks~\cite{A90 (see also~\cite[Chapter 7.5.1]{HSLS21 ), highly-efficient concurrent data structures, such as queues~\cite{GLR83,FG91,MA13,FK14,R19,FGM24 and stacks~\cite{PZ18 , and in many other applications~\cite{FM+90,FK83,KMZ84,Pet82 .}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Previous work ~\cite{HSLS21, FK12, SZ00 has provided implementations of \FetchAndAdd\ that alleviate the bottleneck that arises when multiple processes simultaneously perform \FetchAndAdd\ on a single memory location.}
elements.sent-2411-016: They do this by {diffusing the contention through {\em software combining .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: At a high level, active \FetchAndAdd\ operations coordinate on low-contention ancillary variables, combine their operations, and choose a delegate, so that only the delegates contend for the main variable.
elements.sent-2411-018: The delegate then reports its return value to the \FetchAndAdd\ operations waiting on it and the waiting operations use this to calculate their own return values.
elements.sent-2411-019: This \hedit{combining process ensures that both the ancillary and main variables have low contention.}
elements.sent-2411-020: This line of work culminated in a technique called Combining Funnels~\cite{SZ00 , which filters \FetchAndAdd\ operations through several levels of funnels, combining them pair-wise at each funnel \hedit{by leveraging the swap and compare-and-swap primitives.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: While these existing approaches reduce contention on each variable access, they considerably increase the number of variables accessed by a \FetchAndAdd.
elements.sent-2411-022: The additional cost of these accesses outweighs the benefits when there are few concurrent \FetchAndAdd\ operations.
elements.sent-2411-023: Indeed, we see in our experiments that using Combining Funnels is significantly slower than hardware \FAA\ on low thread counts and only slightly outperforms \FAA\ after 100 threads in \FetchAndAdd-heavy workloads (see ~\Cref{fig:count_thr,fig:count_100 in \Cref{sec:experiments ).}}
elements.sent-2411-024: We present a novel way to implement \FetchAndAdd\ that significantly reduces contention while only slightly increasing the number of variables accessed.
elements.sent-2411-025: It is based on a new Aggregating Funnels technique which permits $k$-way combining of \FetchAndAdd\ operations and is efficient for up to $k=25$ in our experiments.
elements.sent-2411-026: This lets us combine more at each level, and use far fewer levels compared to Combining Funnels.
elements.sent-2411-027: In fact, using just one level of Aggregating Funnels yielded the best performance in our experiments.
elements.sent-2411-028: A technique for $k$-way combining was previously proposed by Tang and Yew~\cite{TY90 , but their algorithm is more complex and uses primitives not available on modern machines, such as \op{Fetch\&Add\&Store which atomically performs \FetchAndAdd\ on one memory location and \op{Store on an adjacent memory location.}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: In contrast, our algorithm uses only the widely available \op{Load , \op{Store , and \FAA\ primitives.}}
elements.sent-2411-030: We refer to the mechanism used to achieve $k$-way combining in our algorithm as an aggregator.
elements.sent-2411-031: Aggregators achieve fast combining by having each thread register itself in a batch using a single \FAA\ instruction.
elements.sent-2411-032: This \FAA\ contends only with other threads mapped to the same aggregator and it serves multiple purposes.
elements.sent-2411-033: It is used to (1) decide the delegate for each batch, (2) sum all of the operations within the batch, (3) determine when the batch is closed, and (4) help determine the return value of the \FetchAndAdd.
elements.sent-2411-034: Previous combining techniques~\cite{HSLS21, FK12, SZ00 use several variables to coordinate these tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Accomplishing all these tasks with a single \FAA\ per operation is one reason for the increased efficiency of our combining approach.
elements.sent-2411-036: Our experimental evaluation shows that Aggregating Funnels start outperforming hardware \FetchAndAdd\ for as few as 30 threads and are up to 4x faster than both Combining Funnels and hardware \FetchAndAdd\ at high thread counts.
elements.sent-2411-037: We prove our \FetchAndAdd\ implementation is strongly linearizable \cite{GHW11 , making it suitable for deployment even in randomized concurrent algorithms.}
elements.sent-2411-038: It is blocking because combined operations must wait for the delegate to bring back a return value from the main variable, but our experiments show this does not lead to uneven performance of threads.
elements.sent-2411-039: In fact, our implementation provides greater fairness (i.e., it results in more similar throughputs at different threads) than using hardware \FAA\ directly.
elements.sent-2411-040: We also provide a \op{Fetch\&AddDirect operation which can be used by higher priority threads to skip the combining step and go directly to the main variable.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Our experiments show that this direct option significantly increases the \FetchAndAdd\ throughput of the high priority threads without reducing the overall throughput.
elements.sent-2411-042: Our implementation is RMWable \cite{JJJ24 , meaning it also supports any other operation that is provided as an atomic primitive.}
elements.sent-2411-043: For example, if the hardware provides compare-and-swap instructions, then a \op{Compare\&Swap can also be supported by our fetch-and-add object.}
elements.sent-2411-044: We believe Aggregating Funnels can be plugged into many applications that use \FAA\ to make them more scalable.
elements.sent-2411-045: As evidence, we \hedit{use them to implement the highly contended fetch-and-add objects in LCRQ, published in PPoPP 2013~\cite{MA13 and recently shown to still be the fastest concurrent queue~\cite{RK23 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: Using our Aggregating Funnels \hedit{eliminates the scalability bottleneck in LCRQ and improves LCRQ's throughput by up to 2.5x for high thread counts.}
elements.sent-2411-047: This speed-up also highlights how the significant efficiency gains observed in the microbenchmarks indeed translate into better performing higher-level applications.. {\bf Our Contributions.}
elements.sent-2411-049: We design the {\em Aggregating Funnels algorithm, which uses hardware \FAA\ instructions to implement \FetchAndAdd\ operations with greatly reduced contention on individual memory locations.}
elements.sent-2411-050: It also provides greater fairness to threads.
elements.sent-2411-051: We implement various versions of our Aggregating Funnels, and empirically show that they provide more scalable \FetchAndAdd\ operations than hardware \FAA\ and the state-of-the-art Combining Funnel algorithm \cite{SZ00 .}
elements.sent-2411-052: Our experiments show that replacing hardware \FAA\ by our Aggregating Funnels makes the fastest available concurrent queue, LCRQ \cite{MA13 , significantly faster and more scalable.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Our implementation is strongly linearizable \cite{GHW11 , making it suitable as a replacement for hardware \FetchAndAdd\ in both deterministic and randomized algorithms.}
elements.sent-2411-054: Our implementation supports all hardware primitives.
elements.sent-2411-055: Similarly, \FetchAndAdd\ implements a timestamp object supporting operations \op{GetTimestamp (), which returns a unique timestamp, and \op{Compare ($\x{ts , \x{ts â€™$), which returns True if \x{ts was obtained earlier than $\x{ts â€™$ and False otherwise.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: Many papers have focused on designing practical implementations of \FetchAndAdd.
elements.sent-2411-057: One approach uses a complete tree of height $\Theta(\log p)$, assigning a leaf to each of the $p$ threads.
elements.sent-2411-058: Each tree node stores some metadata (including a counter).
elements.sent-2411-059: To execute a \FetchAndAdd($diff$), a thread first increments the counter of its leaf by $diff$.
elements.sent-2411-060: Then, it works its way up the tree to the root, updating all nodes of the path it traverses.
elements.sent-2411-061: Every tree node contains a lock.
elements.sent-2411-062: Threads compete for this lock to ascend from a node to its parent, and only the winning thread proceeds to the next level up the tree.
elements.sent-2411-063: The other thread waits for the winner to apply its operation.
elements.sent-2411-064: Combining trees were criticized as having performance that is sensitive to changes in the arrival rate of operations~\cite{HBS95,SZ96,SZ00 : whenever only a subset of the threads is concurrently active, little combining occurs, yet threads must still pay the cost of going through all $\Theta(\log p)$ levels to reach the root.}
elements.sent-2411-065: Combining Funnels~\cite{SZ00 addressed this by replacing the static tree with a series of combining layers, through which \FetchAndAdd\ operations are funnelled.}
elements.sent-2411-066: By using an adaptive scheme, the funnel can change width and depth to accommodate dynamic access patterns.
elements.sent-2411-067: Combining funnels have been experimentally shown~\cite{SZ00 to outperform all schemes discussed in this section that aim to provide low-contention implementations of \FetchAndAdd.}
elements.sent-2411-068: Can we succinctly say how they are related to FAA? Counting networks~\cite{AHS94,M00 can be used to count concurrently and asynchronously.}
elements.sent-2411-069: They are constructed from simple two-input, two-output computing elements called {\em balancers , connected to one another by wires.}
elements.sent-2411-070: Threads traverse different paths through the network and obtain a return value when they reach an output wire of the network.
elements.sent-2411-071: A drawback of this approach is that linearizability cannot be supported without paying a significant cost or by sacrificing other desirable properties~\cite{HSW96, FH04 .}
elements.sent-2411-072: Diffracting trees~\cite{SZ96 employ some form of tree-like counting networks and attempt to reduce contention using arrays ({\em prisms ) where threads attempt to meet and combine.}}
elements.sent-2411-073: They achieve better performance than the counting networks in~\cite{AHS94 , but are not linearizable.}
elements.sent-2411-074: Counting networks and diffracting trees, even when they are used to implement \op{Fetch\&Inc , are less efficient than combining funnels \cite{SZ00 .}}
elements.sent-2411-075: More theoretical work aims at efficiently implementing objects (including fetch-and-add) from other primitives, such as \CAS\ and LL/SC.
elements.sent-2411-076: Jayanti \cite{Jay02 used a technique known as {\em double refresh (originally proposed in~\cite{ADT95 ), to build $f$-arrays, where $f$ is a fixed function over the elements of an array $A[1..n]$; each thread $i$ can update $A[i]$ and query the value of $f$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-077: An $f$-array can be used to get a wait-free implementation of a concurent counter (supporting \op{Add and \op{Read ), whose step complexity is $O(\log p)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-078: Ellen and Woelfel~\cite{EW13 presented a wait-free, linearizable implementation of a Fetch\&Add object with $O(\log p)$ worst-case step complexity from $O(\log m)$-bit registers and LL/SC objects for up to $m$ \FetchAndAdd\ operations.}
elements.sent-2411-079: These papers do not aim at reducing contention and they all use the standard notion of step complexity, which simply counts the maximum number of accesses to shared variables that an operation performs, without taking into account the contention that these accesses may cause.
elements.sent-2411-080: Jayanti~\cite{J98 proved an $\Omega(\log p)$ lower bound on the expected step complexity of any randomized wait-free, linearizable implementation of a single-shot \op{Fetch\&Inc object from \op{LL/SC objects (for a strong adaptive adversary).}}}
elements.sent-2411-081: This bound also holds for long-lived objects, even with amortization \cite{JTB19, Jay22 .}
elements.sent-2411-082: Randomized implementations of \op{Fetch\&Inc with polylogarithmic step complexity are known~\cite{AA+11 .}}
elements.sent-2411-083: State-of-the-art implementations of concurrent queues employ \FAA~\cite{MA13,R19,RK23 .}
elements.sent-2411-084: There is empirical evidence \cite{RK23 that LCRQ~\cite{MA13 has the best performance.}}
elements.sent-2411-085: LCRQ is inspired by the simple idea of using an infinite array, $Q$, and two indices \x{Tail and \x{Head that are updated using \op{Fetch\&Inc .}}}
elements.sent-2411-086: Initially, all elements of $Q$ contain $\bot$, and $\x{Head =\x{Tail =0$.}}
elements.sent-2411-087: To enqueue an item, a thread performs a \op{Fetch\&Inc on \x{Tail to get an index $i$ and tries to swap the item into $Q[i]$ using a \op{Fetch\&Store .}}}
elements.sent-2411-088: Similarly, a dequeue executes \op{Fetch\&Inc on \x{Head to get an index $i$ and tries to remove an item from $Q[i]$ by swapping in the value $\top$.}}
elements.sent-2411-089: This way, each element of $Q$ is accessed by at most one enqueuer and one dequeuer.
elements.sent-2411-090: To bound space usage, LCRQ uses a linked list of circular arrays in place of the infinite array $Q$.
elements.sent-2411-091: LCRQ is lock-free but uses double-word \CAS.
elements.sent-2411-092: LPRQ~\cite{RK23 is a variant of LCRQ that uses single-word \CAS, but it does not outperform LCRQ in empirical tests.}
elements.sent-2411-093: Recent work~\cite{YMC16,NR22 provides wait-free concurrent queues based on \FAA, but they also do not outperform LCRQ in experimental analyses.}

elements.sent-2411-553: In all three scenarios shown in the figure, LCRQ with Aggregating Funnels has up to 2.5x higher throughput than LCRQ with hardware \FAA, and more than 3.5x higher throguhput than LCRQ with Combining Funnels for high thread counts.
elements.sent-2411-554: In this paper, we designed the Aggregating Funnel algorithm for fetch-and-adds.
elements.sent-2411-555: Our microbenchmarks show that Aggregating Funnels are very effective at dissipating contention: outperforming hardware fetch-and-add and the state-of-the-art Combining Funnels algorithm over a variety of workloads.
elements.sent-2411-556: We demonstrated that the speed-ups observed in the microbenchmarks translate to higher-level applications by deploying our Aggregating Funnels in LCRQ.
elements.sent-2411-557: Replacing the hardware fetch-and-add objects with our Aggregating Funnels yields a significant (up to $2.5$x) speed-up in the performance of this state-of-the-art concurrent queue.
elements.sent-2411-558: This work opens up many interesting avenues for future exploration, including: (1) {\em Adapting the algorithm to new settings.}
